coredata.dc:description: Metal/coating systems of marine energy equipment exposed to both environmental erosion and mechanical loads are susceptible to microdamage and localized corrosion, resulting in premature failure of the metal equipment and potentially catastrophic incidents. In-situ monitoring of coating cracking and interfacial corrosion is critically important for health status yet remains a challenge to realize. Herein, a facial and robust damage-sensing strategy based on dynamic fluorescence of carbon quantum dots is proposed to construct smart anticorrosive coatings with hierarchical damage visualization capabilities. Through integrating fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) into an epoxy matrix, the stress-induced crack initiation and propagation dynamics on coating are visually indicated by the intensified fluorescence. Meanwhile, localized metal corrosion at the damaged interface is manifested by ferric ion-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs. The damage responsiveness of smart coating under sustained load and instantaneous impact stress is demonstrated by the dynamic “on-off” behaviors of interfacial fluorescence. Moreover, the toughness and the ability to resist crack generation and corrosion inhibition of coating are significantly enhanced by regulating resin molecular structures. In addition, electrochemical in-situ evaluation was used to assess the failure of coatings in metal/coating systems under stress. This smart coating with hierarchical damage-warning and mechanical properties opens up a new way for on-site, large-scale, and real-time monitoring of microcrack generation and corrosion pits in marine structural components.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: 4-Aminosalicylic acid (ASA, 98\u202F%), isophorone diamine (IPDA) and polyether amine D400 were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Epoxy resin (E51) was supplied by Marine Chemical Research Institute. Sodium chloride (NaCl) and ethanol (C 2 H 6 O) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Mild steel (Q235) was selected as the metal substrate for anticorrosion investigation, which was supported by Shengxin Technology Co. Before preparing the coating, the Q235 substrate was polished to 1500 grit, which was then rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, and subsequently dried in warm air.

head.ce:title: Dynamic visualization for stress-induced crack initiation and resulted corrosion expansion in carbon quantum tailored epoxy coating

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal/coating systems of marine energy equipment exposed to both environmental erosion and mechanical loads are susceptible to microdamage and localized corrosion, resulting in premature failure of the metal equipment and potentially catastrophic incidents. In-situ monitoring of coating cracking and interfacial corrosion is critically important for health status yet remains a challenge to realize. Herein, a facial and robust damage-sensing strategy based on dynamic fluorescence of carbon quantum dots is proposed to construct smart anticorrosive coatings with hierarchical damage visualization capabilities. Through integrating fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) into an epoxy matrix, the stress-induced crack initiation and propagation dynamics on coating are visually indicated by the intensified fluorescence. Meanwhile, localized metal corrosion at the damaged interface is manifested by ferric ion-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs. The damage responsiveness of smart coating under sustained load and instantaneous impact stress is demonstrated by the dynamic “on-off” behaviors of interfacial fluorescence. Moreover, the toughness and the ability to resist crack generation and corrosion inhibition of coating are significantly enhanced by regulating resin molecular structures. In addition, electrochemical in-situ evaluation was used to assess the failure of coatings in metal/coating systems under stress. This smart coating with hierarchical damage-warning and mechanical properties opens up a new way for on-site, large-scale, and real-time monitoring of microcrack generation and corrosion pits in marine structural components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is ubiquitously existed in every metallic object and is a notorious problem for the structural integrity and maintenance of marine engineering equipment including cross-sea bridges, marine ships, offshore wind power facilities, and harbour engineering structures. [1–3] Polymeric coatings are regarded as the most efficacious and cost-effective strategy for corrosion protection of marine metallic equipment by forming a barrier that isolates them from corrosive mediums and oxygen. [4,5] Among the various coating materials, epoxy coatings are the most prevalent due to their high adhesion, excellent mechanical strength and superior chemical stability. [6] However, marine protective coatings are subjected to synergistic coupling impact of wave scouring, sediment, current and thermal stress load, which promote the generation of coating microdamage and thus initiate the occurrence of interfacial corrosion. [7] However, coating damage, especially small-scale pores or cracks are with crypticity and difficult to precisely detected, which could develop into macro-damage under mechanical stress and corrosion conditions, and ultimately compromise coating integrity and protective performance. [8] Therefore, it is highly pursued to design build-in sensing units in smart coatings that can visually detect coating microdamage generation and interfacial substrate corrosion, and monitor the dynamic failure process of coatings.

Self-monitoring (SM) or self-warning (SW) coatings are capable of converting material failure events to visual signals, exhibiting damage information about materials deterioration. [9,10] Coating damage usually originates under external mechanical force or internal stresses, so mechanochromic polymers have been designed and developed to detect material damage. [11] To achieve the highly sensitive perception of stress, it is essential to position the force induced chromotropic unit at an appropriate point within the polymer network. Nevertheless, the intricacy of the synthesis process and the reliance on the matrix resin present significant challenges that limit their suitability as ideal SM materials. An alternative approach is to implement a damage monitoring function by embedding nanocontainers or microcapsules containing color developers or dyes into the coating. Damage to the coating causes the embedded microcapsules to rupture, resulting in the release of the internal color-developing material and achieving damage visualization through apparent chromogenic behavior. [12,13] However, the sensitivity of microcapsules based SM coatings is significantly constrained by the limited capacity to encapsulate functional molecules, the unsatisfied interfacial compatibility with the polymer resin, and the discrepancy between the force that causes the capsules to rupture and the force that causes the coating to damage. The aforementioned SW coatings are mainly focused on the coating damage while the corrosion pits at coating/substrate interface are difficult to be visually monitored. In marine environments, corrosion of the metal substrate at the coating micro-damaged area occurs rapidly with the accumulation of corrosion products, accelerating crack expansion and propagation, ultimately leading to the destruction of coating adhesion. Therefore, it is imperative to monitor the early corrosion generation and report the coating degradation extent.

To determine the interfacial corrosion behavior and evaluate the failure evolution mechanism of a coating system, smart corrosion SM coatings have been constructed through integrating corrosion sensitive motifs. Generally, metal corrosion is complex redox process, which results in physical or chemical alterations in the microenvironment by the uptake and release of metal ions and local pH. [14] The majority of existing corrosion SM coatings are based on the chromogenic reaction of metallic ions or pH indicators, which can convert the microscopic corrosion process into macroscopic visual signals, thereby providing corrosion dynamic and structural health conditions. [15] More recently, Cheng and colleagues developed a new self-warning coating with hierarchical warnings of coating damage and interfacial corrosion by using mesoporous polydopamine nanospheres loaded with crystalline violet lactone and phenanthroline molecules. [16] However, the generated two damage response signals resulting from different molecules interfere with each other, making it difficult to distinguish the failure extent of the coating system. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are nanoscale carbon-based materials with high surface area, abundant functional groups, and excellent photoluminescence properties. The surface functional groups of CDs can coordinate with metal ions accompanied by attenuation in fluorescence intensity of CDs, ensuring CDs act as promising candidates for fabricating smart corrosion detecting coatings. [17] Although extensive efforts have been implemented on corrosion monitoring and damage detection, the current focus is only concentrates on feedback from specific types of damage. However, in practical conditions, it is inevitable that coatings materials suffer from complex mechanical stress (tensile, bending and compression), resulting in irregular crack initiation and uncertain damage propagation of coatings. Consequently, in-situ monitoring of coating damage occurrence and failure dynamics of coating systems under mechanical stress are highly important yet remains challenge to achieve.

Herein, we report a new generation of damage SM coatings with notable coating crack warning and localized corrosion sensing capabilities, fabricated by the rational integration of synthesized N-CDs and epoxy resin, as shown in Fig. 1a. Under mechanical action, the stress-induced crack initiation and dynamic extension in the coating can produce enhanced fluorescence in the fractured region, which assists the refringence and resists internal reflection. Meanwhile, the presented N-CDs can act as corrosion probe, exhibiting fluorescence quenching phenomenon at localized corroded regions, thus presenting corrosion monitoring function. Moreover, the mechanical toughness and damage resistance of composite coating can be greatly improved through strategic molecular engineering of epoxy network. In addition, the interfacial failure dynamics of steel/coating systems under stress were investigated using a novel homemade mechanical-electrochemical test device. Consequently, the integration of crack monitoring and corrosion sensing functionality provides a novel, facial and robust strategy to achieve damage warning and corrosion inhibiting, exhibiting great potential in harsh marine anticorrosion engineering regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: 4-Aminosalicylic acid (ASA, 98 %), isophorone diamine (IPDA) and polyether amine D400 were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Epoxy resin (E51) was supplied by Marine Chemical Research Institute. Sodium chloride (NaCl) and ethanol (C2H6O) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Mild steel (Q235) was selected as the metal substrate for anticorrosion investigation, which was supported by Shengxin Technology Co. Before preparing the coating, the Q235 substrate was polished to 1500 grit, which was then rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, and subsequently dried in warm air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, N-CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method using aminosalicylic acid (ASA) as a precursor. [17] Specifically, 0.5 g of ASA was first ultrasonically dissolved in 50 mL of ethanol and then transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclaves, which were heated at 200 °C for 18 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the obtained dark brown solution was purified using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of ~3000 D for 2 d. Finally, black solid (N-CDs) was obtained by drying in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational methods
ce:section.ce:para: GaussView 5.0 and Gaussian 09w software were employed to optimize the partial structures of N-CDs and N-CDs-Fe3+ molecules. The optimized structures were achieved by adjusting the size of the N-CDs according to the size of the benzene ring (0.28 nm) of the selected model. Density-functional theory (DFT) based on the b3lyp exchange-correlation function and the standard basis set 6-31G(d) was employed for the molecular simulations. The structures were then optimized using Gaussian 09w to obtain the optimal structure; meanwhile, the electrostatic form and the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) were also obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CDs tailored smart coating (CDs/EP)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the composite coating, the synthesized N-CDs powder (4.2 × 10−4 g) was initially dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol by ultrasonication. Subsequently, 10 μL of the aforementioned solution was added to 0.85 g of IPDA and mechanically stirred for a period of 5 min to ensure thorough mixing. Thereafter, 4 g of E51 epoxy resin was added and mechanically stirred for a further 10 min. To guarantee the coating's density, any residual air bubbles were removed in a vacuum oven. Subsequently, the resin mixture was applied to the pretreated Q235 substrate via a wire rod applicator, ensuring uniform coverage. The coated samples were then subjected to a curing process in an oven at 70 °C for 9 h, resulting in a composite coating with a thickness of 70 ± 5 μm, designated as the CDs/EI coating. In comparison, CDs/ED coating (where the ED coating consists of E51 and D400 with a mass ratio of 2:1) was also prepared according to the same process. In addition, the coating thickness was evaluated using a coating thickness gauge (Table S1). The mechanical properties of the materials were studied by pouring the EI coating into dumbbell and rectangular polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) molds to prepare tensile and three-point bending test samples, respectively. Subsequently, both test samples were cured in an oven at 70 °C for 9 h to obtain fully cured coatings. The ED coating samples were prepared according to the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the synthesized N-CDs was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM, MULTIMODE8, Bruker Corporation, German) in tapping mode. Furthermore, the chemical structures and functional groups of N-CDs were determined through Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet 380, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA) Raman spectroscopy (DXR2, Thermo Fisher, USA) were employed to confirm the chemical compositions and structural variations of the N-CDs nanomaterials. Furthermore, the optical properties of the synthesized N-CDs and the complexation of N-CDs with Fe3+ were investigated through fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan), UV–visible spectrophotometer (Agilent CARY 60, USA), and Colorimeter (ST70, Shenzhen SUNSHI Technology Co. China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of mechanical properties, damage monitoring and corrosion warning properties of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: The tensile stress test and three-point bending test were performed on an electronic universal testing machine (GT-AI 7000S, China) according to the standard strain-stress test. The test conditions for the tensile stress test were a deformation rate of 50 mm/min, and for the three-point bending test, the test condition was a deformation rate of 5 mm/min.

To investigate the damage responsiveness of the synthesized smart anti-corrosion coatings, cracks were created on the surface of the coatings using a homemade deflection application device and a drop hammer impact tester, respectively. The fluorescence emission intensity was measured before and after the appearance of damage using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (excitation wavelength of 365 nm) and a colorimeter. Additionally, crack extension behavior on the coating surface was observed using a metallurgical microscope (OM, Axio Lab A1, ZEISS, Germany).

In order to investigate the corrosion responsiveness of smart anticorrosion coatings, the cracked coated samples described above were immersed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to initiate interfacial corrosion. The fluorescence variations in the defective area were recorded and analyzed through fluorescence spectrophotometer and Colorimeter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of electrochemical behavior of coated samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the corrosion electrochemical behavior of coatings under applied stress, a homemade in-situ mechano-electrochemical test setup was utilized. The configuration of the working electrode of the in-situ mechano-electrochemical test setup is depicted in Fig. S1a. As can be seen from the figure, a waterproof adhesive was used to encapsulate the coating and the metal substrate, and an area of 1 cm2 was reserved for electrochemical testing. As illustrated in Fig. S1b, the in situ mechano-electrochemical test setup comprises a pressure transducer, a deflection application device, an electrochemical workstation, and a computer. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) utilizing a classical three-electrode system. The coated samples act as the working electrode, while a platinum sheet with an area of 1 cm2 serves as the counter electrode, and the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) accounts for the reference electrode The entire electrochemical device was operated in a Faraday cage to shield it from interference. The EIS signals were captured in the frequency sweep ranging from 10−2-105 Hz with a 20 mV of sinusoidal voltage amplitude. After EIS tests, the surface morphology and corrosive components were explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova NanoSEM 450, FEI, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Result and discussion
Synthesis mechanism and characterization of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N-CDs were synthesized from ASA and ethanol via the solvothermal method, which was schematically illustrated in Fig. 1a. The chemical reactions that may occur during the synthesis of N-CDs are schematically illustrated in Fig. S2. These reactions include the condensation reaction between the carboxyl group (-COOH) and the amino group (−NH2) in ASA, which forms an amide bond (-CO-NH-) or oligomer; the esterification reaction between the carboxyl group (-COOH) in ASA and the hydroxyl group (-OH) in ethanol, which forms an esterified product; and the substitution reaction between the hydroxyl groups (-OH) in ASA, which forms an oligomer. Besides, the resulting N-CDs present black solid powder, as exhibited in Fig. S3a. After being dispersed in water, uniform gray dispersion was observed in Fig. S3b, demonstrating its good dispersibility in water. Upon irradiation with UV lamps, the aqueous solution of N-CDs exhibits a notable blue-green fluorescence, indicating that it possesses favorable fluorescence responsiveness (Fig. S3c). Furthermore, upon illumination with a white-light lamp (Fig. S3d), the Tyndall effect was discernible, substantiating the colloidal properties of N-CDs.

The morphology and size of N-CDs were investigated using atomic force microscopy. As is shown in Fig. 2a and b, the typical AFM images and the corresponding height distribution of N-CDs revealed an approximately spherical shape with a size distribution of 2–6 nm. [18] For the large size product in the figure is due to agglomeration during sample preparation. The carbon structure of the N-CDs was examined through Raman spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the synthesized N-CDs exhibit two distinct peaks: the G-band (sp2-hybridization) at 1577 cm−1 and the D-band (sp3-hybridization) at 1372 cm−1. The D-band represents the low-carbon lattice structure (degree of defects) of the carbon material, while the G-band is the band of the optical energy level of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. [17,19,20] These peaks are significantly different from those of ASA, which is related to the solvothermal reaction that occurs during the synthesis. Overall, it can be demonstrated that the synthesized N-CDs have a disordered graphite-like structure. Besides the functional groups in ASA and N-CDs during the synthesis process were investigated using FT-IR measurement, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. For ASA, the typical absorption peaks presented at 3496 cm−1 and 3390 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds, respectively. [21] Meanwhile, the characteristic stretching vibrations of the CO (3000 cm−1) and CH bonds (2534 cm−1), [22] and the vibration of the NH bond (1623 cm−1) [23] and the bending vibrations of the CN (1452 cm−1) and CO (1299 cm−1) bonds were observed respectively. [19,24] For N-CD, the weak peak near 3370 cm−1 represents unreacted OH and NH groups. The CO peak disappears due to decomposition, intermolecular cyclization and condensation that would occur as a result of solvent heat treatment. [25] It is noteworthy that a new peak at 1621 cm−1 appears, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the -CO-NH- bond. This is due to the amidation of the carboxyl and amino groups in the ASA. [26] In addition, the peak at 1490 cm−1 was identified as a CC vibration, which is a unique functional group resulting from the carbonation of N-CDs, [27] indicating the successful synthesis of N-CDs. Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to analyze the chemical composition and structures of N-CDs. The complete XPS spectrum of N-CDs is shown in Fig. 2e, which shows that N-CDs are composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The detailed information about the chemical valence of N-CDs was further determined by fitting the XPS spectra. C1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) exhibits three chemical shift peak components at 284.6, 285.7, and 289.1 eV, which are attributed to CC, C-O/C-N, and C=N/C=O, respectively. [28,29] The fine structure spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 2g) was fitted into two peaks, including C-N-C (396.1 eV) and (C)3N (397.1 eV). [30] It is noteworthy that the presence of C-N-C indicates the amidation reaction between -COOH and NH2, demonstrating the successful preparation of N-CDs.

The optical properties of N-CDs were examined by UV–visible spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2h. A broad but weak absorption peak at about 487–530 nm is attributed to the n-π* leap of C=N/C=O or CN. [31] In addition, the photoluminescence properties of the N-CDs were further demonstrated by fluorescence spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. 2i, with the increase in the excitation wavelength from 365 nm to 420 nm, the emission wavelength is stabilized at 480 nm with high fluorescence intensity, which indicates excellent fluorescence responsive properties of the synthesized N-CDs. The fluorescence excitation spectra of N-CDs are shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence responsiveness of CDs is attributed to the abundant surface chemical functional groups [32–34] and surface states, [35,36] presenting potential in materials damage detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ responsiveness and fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the interaction between the surface functional groups of N-CDs and Fe3+, UV–vis spectroscopy of N-CDs solutions (42 μg/mL) with varying Fe3+ concentrations was examined. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the introduction of Fe3+ resulted in the emergence of a pronounced absorption band at 455 nm. This phenomenon was ascribed to the robust charge transfer absorption between Fe3+ and ligand, thereby substantiating the coordination of synthesized N-CDs with Fe3+. In addition, to further determine the complexation situation of N-CDs and Fe3+, the reflection spectra of N-CDs with different contents of Fe3+ were detected. As presented in Fig. 3b, the reflectance curves exhibit a gradual decline trend with increase of Fe3+ concentration, indicating a concomitant change in the color of the N-CDs solution, which becomes progressively darker. This phenomenon is also evident in the image captured in visible light mode (Fig. 3f), where the color gradually deepens with increasing Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 3c displays the color aberration of N-CDs solutions by incremental adjustment of Fe3+ concentrations. It can be observed that the color aberration increases in tandem with the degree of complexation, exhibiting an approximate linear correlation with Fe3+ concentration. These observations evidenced that the synthesized N-CDs can serve as efficient Fe3+ probe to visually monitor the steel corrosion process.

Moreover, to investigate the fluorescence responsiveness of N-CDs to Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution with various contents of Fe3+ was also determined. Fig. 3d illustrates the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs following the addition of varying concentrations of Fe3+ (wavelength of 365 nm). For pure N-CDs solution, an apparent luminescence emission peak was exhibited at 480 nm. With the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually declines. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution was completely quenched when the Fe3+ concentration reached 2.5 μg/mL, attributing to the complete coordination of N-CDs with Fe3+ Accordingly, the fluorescence images of the above solutions under UV lamps were presented in Fig. 3f. In the absence of Fe3+, the solution exhibited blue-green fluorescence, which diminished and ultimately quenched with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. The UV-emitting fluorescent analog color of N-CDs–Fe3+ complex was then recorded in CIE chromaticity coordinates corresponding to the fluorescence transition of the complex in optical photographs (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculation of fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs- Fe3+ coordinates
ce:section.ce:para:
To reveal the mechanism of fluorescence quenching behavior of N-CDs- Fe3+ coordinates, Gaussian 09 W and GaussView 5.0 were employed. Gaussian software based on density functional theory was used to simulate and calculate part of the molecular structures of N-CDs and the N-CDs-Fe3+ complex. Accordingly, the optimized molecular structures, electrostatic formula energies, HOMO, LUMO and energy gap were obtained. Figs. 4a1 and 4a2 show the theoretical partial structures of N-CDs and N-CDs-Fe3+ respectively, which are composed of internal carbon cores and abundant functional groups on the surface (-OH, -COOH, -NH2, =CH2, etc.). Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) is a well-established idea that provides detailed data on the electrophilic and nucleophilic reaction sites of a compound. [37] The electrostatic formulas of N-CDs before and after complexing with Fe3+ are given in Figs. 4b1 and 4b2, respectively. Electron-poor regions of the MEP surface are depicted in blue, while electron-rich regions are shown in red. [38] The order of increasing electron density on the MEP surface is red>orange>yellow>green>blue. [39] As presented in Fig. 4b2 , the Fe3+coordinated range shows a darker blue color compared with the non-complexed part, indicating the lowered electron density. In this situation, the N-CDs are more likely to be repelled by Fe3+, reducing the possibility of reaction and ensuring the stability of complex. [40] Besides, the HOMO and LUMO energy and the size of the energy gap can be considered as effective parameters to evaluate the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of CDs to a certain extent. [41] PLQY can be calculated using the following formula:
PLQY =
N em
/
N abs
× 100 %
Among them, Nem represents the number of photons emitted, and Nabs represents the number of photons absorbed.

To investigate the alteration of the energy gap subsequent to the complexation reaction of Fe3+ with N-CDs, the energies of the energy levels were calculated before and after the complexation. Fig. 4c shows the calculated HOMO and LUMO orbitals of N-CDs and N-CDs-Fe3+ complex. The energy values of N-CDs and N-CDs-Fe3+ are presented in Fig. 4c. The energy gaps (ΔE = ELUMO-EHOMO) between the HOMO and LUMO energy levels are calculated. As exhibited, N-CDs-Fe3+ coordinates possess lower ΔE value (2.267 eV) than that of N-CDs (3.321 eV), indicating that the energy gap is reduced after the complexation reaction. This phenomenon can be attributed to the complexation reaction of Fe3+ with the surface functional groups of N-CDs, which results in the twisting and deformation of the aforementioned surface functional groups. This, in turn, gives rise to a small spatial orbital overlap between the HOMO energy level and the LUMO energy level, which ultimately leads to a reduction in the energy gap. [42] It is noteworthy that the presence of Fe3+ will facilitate non-radiative transitions, whereby the excited electrons are transferred to the d orbitals of Fe3+ within the molecule, inducing intramolecular vibrations and releasing them in the form of heat energy. This reduces the PLQY. [43] Since the Fe3+ readily coordinates with the -OH, -COOH and -NH2 groups on N-CDs, and the fluorescent “on-off” mechanism of the N-CDs, [44] the possible complexation mechanism of the fluorescent nanomaterials N-CDs was shown in Fig. 4d. Specifically, in the presence of Fe3+, the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs that can stimulate fluorescence will complex with them, thereby altering the structure of these functional groups. This process also promotes non-radiative transition, inhibits radiative transition, and ultimately quenches the fluorescence. Therefore, based on the fluorescence quenching effect, N-CDs can be used as a sensitive probe for the detection of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and toughening treatment of epoxy coating
ce:section.ce:para: The deformation capability and crack resistance of polymers closely relate to the resin network structure and mechanical properties. [45,46] Bisphenol-A type epoxy resin has been widely applied for constructing protective coatings with excellent comprehensive performance through forming tight three-dimensional network structure. However, the intrinsic brittleness, insufficient crack resistance, and poor energy dissipation properties impede its practical applications. [47] Therefore, two epoxy coatings were designed by introducing molecular chains with different structures to increase the toughness and energy dissipation ability of resulting composite coatings, which were illustrated in Fig. 5a. The curing reaction is a nucleophilic addition reaction between the amine groups in the amine curing agent and the epoxy groups in the epoxy resin. The epoxy group will be opened to produce a reactive carbocation, which forms a nucleophilic addition reaction with the amine group to generate an intermediate product. The intermediate product continues to react with other epoxy resin molecules or its curing agent molecules, resulting in a cross-nucleophilic addition reaction and ultimately forming a three-dimensional cross-linked network structure. Among them, the IPDA is a hard curing agent, [48] while D400 is a flexible construction primitive. [49] The cross-linked networks generated by the reaction of the two curing agents with the epoxy resin are schematically illustrated in Fig. 5b.

To further investigate the toughness of the composite epoxy coatings after modulation of the molecular chains, tensile tests were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the EI coating exhibited brittle fracture at relatively low strains, indicating their limited deformation capacity. In contrast, the ED coating demonstrated enhanced flexibility due to the introduction of the D400 flexible molecular chain, allowing it to undergo a larger strain under stress and display a necking phase. The specific values of elongation at break and tensile strength for both coatings are presented in Fig. S5. As displayed in Fig. 5e, the toughness values of the two coatings are markedly disparate, with the ED coating exhibiting a considerably higher level of resilience than the EI coating. The results of the tensile experiments indicate that the mechanical properties of the composite coatings are enhanced by the introduction of D400 flexible molecular chains. Furthermore, three-point bending experiments were conducted to investigate the fracture toughness of the composite coatings. As evidenced by the load-deflection curves (Fig. 5d), the maximum deflection and destructive load that the EI coating can withstand in three-point bending tests are 3.49 mm and 0.18 MPa, respectively. In comparison, ED coatings demonstrate the capacity to withstand maximum deflection and breaking loads of 12.26 mm and 0.14 MPa, respectively. The results of the three-point bending test demonstrate that the incorporation of D400 molecular chains can enhance the fracture toughness of the composite coating. In conclusion, modifying the molecular structure of the coating can increase toughness and enhance energy dissipation capacity, thereby minimizing damage to the coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adhesion properties of coatings to Q235 substrates before and after N-CDs addition
ce:section.ce:para: For corrosion protective coatings, the crack initiation, expansion and peeling behaviors under stress closely relate to the adhesion strength between the polymeric coating and metallic substrate. In order to investigate the adhesive properties of different coatings, pull-off tests were conducted to simulate the damage of normal force to adhered systems based on ASTM D4541 standard. As shown in Fig. S6, the introduction of synthesized CDs improved the adhesion strength in comparison to the pure EI and ED coatings. Furthermore, the peeling areas of both CDs/EI and CDs/ED composite coatings obviously reduced, indicating the lower failure level of cohesion and adhesion. The enhanced interfacial bonding is attributed to the abundant functional groups present on the surface of N-CDs, which enhanced the interfacial interaction between the coating and the substrate, as well as between the molecular chains of the resin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Damage visualization of N-CDs tailored coating under instantaneous stress
ce:section.ce:para: It is expected to visualize the micro-damages of coating during impact of external environments. To examine the damage monitoring performance of prepared CDs/EP coating, a transient stress was applied on the two coating samples. The impact resistance of the two coatings was initially evaluated through a drop hammer impact test. As evidenced in Fig. 6a1 , the CDs/EI coating demonstrates a range of failure states following exposure to varying heights of falling hammer impacts. When the height of the falling hammer is less than 300 mm, the cracks on the surface of the coating gradually expand with the increase of the height of the falling hammer. At a height of 500 mm, the coating begins to peel off, and at 700 mm, the coating is completely peeled off. In the case of the CDs/EI coating (Fig. S7a), the surface of the coating remained smooth following impact by the falling hammer at 700 mm, and no macroscopic damage was observed. To gain further insight into the expansion of microcracks in the coatings, a metallurgical microscope was employed to observe the two coatings after they had been subjected to impact. As shown in Fig. 6a2 , the surface cracks of the CDs/EI coating exhibited a linear expansion in all directions from the center of the force, akin to a “spiderweb” structure, as the height of the hammer increased. The coating subsequently peeled off after being impacted by the hammer at 700 mm, resulting in a catastrophic failure. With regard to the CDs/ED coating (Fig. S7b), it can be observed that as the height of the hammer increases, the outer surface of the coating remains intact, whereas small zigzag cracks emerge within the coating. Upon impact at 700 mm, the coating exhibited a flattening rather than peeling, suggesting that CDs/ED coating demonstrates superior energy dissipation and impact resistance compared to CDs/EI coating. Furthermore, to examine the fluorescence response characteristics of CDs/EP coating, a UV lamp was employed to irradiate the coatings following their exposure to an impact force, and the resulting phenomenon was observed. As illustrated in Fig. 6a3 , the fluorescence at the cracks of the CDs/EI coating is markedly enhanced in comparison to the intact region. However, no fluorescence is observed at the region where the coating is detached, indicating that the coating exhibits excellent fluorescence response performance. In the case of the CDs/ED coating (Fig. S7c), the fluorescence of its surface is consistent with that of the unimpacted region due to the absence of cracks on its surface.

To further investigate the damage sensing property of coatings, the coated samples were subjected to a drop hammer impact with the coating facing upwards, as shown in Fig. S8. For the CDs/EI coating, it can be seen that the surface of the coating appears to be damaged and exhibits a significant fluorescence enhancement at crack when the falling hammer located at 100 mm. As the height of the hammer increases, the cracks on the surface of the coating gradually expand, and when the height of the hammer is at 500 mm, the coating at the impacted area is completely peeled off, showing obvious fluorescence enhancement behavior under UV conditions. For the CDs/ED coating, when it was impacted by the hammer at 100 mm, the coating and the base metal were depressed downward, and the fluorescence intensity of the impacted area was basically the same as that of the unimpacted area under UV conditions, which indicated that the coating was not damaged at this time. When the height of the hammer was 300 mm, the base metal of the coating was obviously depressed, and the impacted area showed fluorescence enhancement under UV conditions, indicating that the coating was damaged, but the cracks in the damaged area did not extend outward compared with the IPDA coating. When the height of the hammer was 500 mm, the coating was obviously damaged and showed obvious fluorescence enhancement under UV conditions. The primary rationale for this phenomenon pertains to the sequence of events that transpire during the impact testing of the coating. In the downward impact test, the hammer initially makes contact with the base metal, which absorbs a portion of the impact energy. Subsequently, the remaining energy is transferred to the coating, which exhibits a reduced capacity to absorb impact energy and consequently experiences less damage. Conversely, in the upward impact test, the falling hammer directly impacts the coating, which absorbs a greater amount of impact energy and consequently sustains more substantial damage.

To elucidate the mechanism of crack-induced fluorescence enhancement, as illustrated in the schematic diagram of Fig. 6b. For CDs/EI coating, the formation of a brittle three-dimensional network structure renders them susceptible to disruption upon impact, resulting in the breakage of molecular chains and the subsequent disintegration of the three-dimensional network. [48] This ultimately leads to the emergence of macroscopic damage and the propagation of cracks. The generation of cracks results in an increase in surface roughness of the coating, which inhibits internal reflection of light and promotes refraction. [50] This ultimately leads to fluorescence enhancement at the cracks. The fluorescence enhancement phenomenon can be observed at the cracks, which can be used to visualize their presence. In the case of the CDs/ED coating, the fluorescence on the surface is not enhanced. This is because the added D400 molecules have flexible molecular chains, forming a three-dimensional network structure that is relatively smooth and has high toughness. Consequently, when subjected to impact forces, these forces are dissipated through deformation, which does not cause the molecular chain and three-dimensional network to fracture. Therefore, there is no fluorescence enhancement phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Damage visualization of N-CDs coating under sustained stress
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the crack initiation behavior and reveal the failure mechanisms of synthetic CDs/EP coating under sustained stress, the surface and interface characteristics of coatings at different deflections were studied by using a homemade static stress loading apparatus. Deflections of 12 mm, 17 mm, and 23 mm were applied to the two samples using the aforementioned apparatus, and the resulting surface fluorescence changes were observed under UV illumination, as shown in Fig. 7a and Fig. S9. No obvious fluorescence variations were observed for both CDs/EI and CDs/ED samples when the deflections reached 12 mm and 17 mm. With the deflection reaching 23 mm, the CDs/EI coating has been stripped from the steel substrate and greatly fractured, revealing the limited deformation ability. An intensified fluorescence presents at the locations of the peeling and the fracture, particularly at the cracked regions. The fluorescence intensity of defected area was markedly different from the initial state, exhibiting a brighter blue fluorescence. The results of the fluorescence spectroscopy also corroborated this phenomenon. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence was most prominent at the crack (III), followed by the peeled area (II), while the intact portion (I) exhibited the lowest fluorescence. Moreover, reflectance spectra were employed to assess the color of the samples, as evidenced in Fig. S10. It can be observed that the fractured region exhibits the highest reflectance and a lighter hue, whereas the compact part displays the lowest reflectance and a darker color. However, there are no cracks and fluorescence enhancement phenomenon displayed on the surface of the CDs/ED coating even after applying 23 mm of deflection, as shown in Fig. S9. Based on the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. S11, the fluctuations in fluorescence intensity at the three positions (I), (II), and (III) are negligible, indicating that no obvious damage occurred in the CDs/ED coating. Furthermore, the fluorescence rate of change curve of the two coatings are illustrated in Fig. 7c. The presence of cracks greatly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/EI coating, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/ED coating remains relatively stable, indicating favorable crack resistance. The damage monitoring and crack warning mechanism of a coating subjected to sustained stress was schematically illustrated in Fig. 7d. A deflection application device is employed to apply stress to the CDs/EP, thereby increasing its deflection and subjecting the coating surface to tensile stress. Once the applied deflection exceeds the coating threshold, the structure of resin networks is damaged, resulting in crack generation and propagation. The existence of cracks or damages increase in coating roughness, which suppresses internal reflection and promotes refraction, leading to enhanced fluorescence. [50] Therefore, the damage location and expansion dynamics can be visually monitored and detected on coating surface, assisting the structural health monitoring of coating system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of smart coatings under sustained stress conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The above investigations demonstrated that the synthesized N-CDs can endow the coating system with efficient damage monitoring and crack visualizing functionalities. For corrosion inhibiting applications, it is essential to determine the electrochemical corrosion and interfacial failure behaviors under mechanical loads. Hence, an in-situ mechanical-electrochemical test device was designed and employed to study the corrosion reactivity of coatings under stress. As previously indicated, cracks can be created on the surface of CDs/EP coating using a homemade deflection application device. In this study, we used this device in combination with an electrochemical workstation (Fig. 8a) to perform an in-situ inspection of the failure process of the coatings under stress.

The EIS results for the CDs/EI coated samples are presented in Figs. 8b-8d and Fig. S12a. It can be observed that the impedance evolution and damage process of the coating under different deflections can be divided into three distinct stages. In the absence of deflection, the first stage is observed to exhibit a Nyquist curve (Fig. S12a) and only observes a large reactance arc. The magnitude of the reactance arc offers a preliminary assessment of the corrosion resistance of the coating. A larger reactance radius is indicative of superior corrosion resistance, whereas a smaller reactance radius is indicative of inferior corrosion resistance. [15] Thus, the Nyquist curve for the first stage indicates that at this point the coating is equivalent to an insulating layer with a high resistance value. In the context of Bode-impedance diagram, the low-frequency impedance modulus (Z f=0.01Hz) is typically employed as a key indicator for assessing barrier performance and the extent of coating failure. [51] As illustrated in Fig. 8b and d, the low-frequency impedance associated with the initial phase is approximately 2.872 × 1011 Ω cm2. Fig. 8c shows that the phase angle in this stage is approximately −90° over a broad range and behaves as a time constant, indicating that the coating at this point corresponds to an isolation layer with a substantial resistance value and a relatively low capacitance value. The corresponding equivalent circuit model is illustrated in Fig. 8h, which includes solution resistance (R s), coating resistance (R c) and coating capacitance (Q c). In consideration of the inhomogeneity of the actual electrodes, a constant phase element (Q) is employed in lieu of a capacitive element (C). [52,53] The applied deflection of 12–17 mm corresponds to the second phase, as illustrated in Fig. 8b. This figure also demonstrates that the radius of the reactance arc in this phase is significantly reduced in comparison to the first phase, indicating a decrease in the barrier capacity of the coating. The Bode-impedance diagram (Fig. 8b and d) in the low-frequency region shows a decrease in the resistance value of approximately 4 × 1010 Ω cm2 with respect to the first phase. Two-time constants are evident in the Bode-phase angle diagram (Fig. 8c). In the high-frequency region, the corresponding time constants are the coating capacitance (Q c) and the coating resistance (R c). In the low-frequency region, the time constants are derived from the double-layer capacitance (Q dl) of the electrolyte penetrating into the coating to reach the substrate metal and the charge-transfer resistance (R ct) where the metal corrosion reaction occurs. [54] At this juncture, the model illustrated in Fig. 8h is no longer a suitable representation and the model depicted in Fig. 8i is employed to more accurately align with the equivalent circuit at this stage. When the applied deflection exceeds 23 mm, this corresponds to the third stage. As illustrated in Fig. S12a, the radius of the arc resistance exhibits a notable decline in comparison to the second stage, suggesting that the bright coating has sustained significant macroscopic damage, resulting in the loss of its barrier capacity. Fig. 8b and d show a five-order-of-magnitude decrease in resistance in the low-frequency region at this stage, compared to the second stage. Fig. 8c depicts three-time constants, with the time constant of the mid-frequency region corresponding to the oxide layer generated by the corrosion reaction of the metal. This indicates that the coating has failed entirely and that severe corrosion has occurred on the metal surface. The circuit model fitted to this stage is shown in Fig. 8j, where R diff and Q diff refer to the resistance and capacitance of the generated iron oxide. [53]

The EIS test results of CDs/ED coating are shown in Fig. 8e-8g. In contrast to the CDs/EI coating, the CDs/ED coating exhibits a single stage of impedance evolution and damage process under varying deflections. As illustrated in Fig. S12b, all Nyquist curves exhibit a single, prominent reactance arc, indicating that the coatings display substantial capacitive behavior as high-resistance insulators. The Bode-impedance diagram (Fig. 8e), the Bode-phase angle diagram (Fig. 8f), and the low-frequency impedance value graph (Fig. 8g) demonstrate that the coating resistance and phase angle remain largely unaltered following the application of varying deflections to the CDs/ED coating samples. This suggests that the coating is resilient to sustained stress and that corrosive media do not penetrate the metal surface. This finding is consistent with the initial phase of CDs/EI coating, for which the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 8h also applies. The impedance parameters of the two coatings when different deflections were applied are listed in Table S2. The results of the EIS tests demonstrated that the CDs/ED coating exhibited no signs of damage when subjected to sustained stress, whereas the CDs/EI coating showed clear signs of damage and failed completely. This suggests that the toughening of the CDs/EP coating was enhanced by the modulation of the molecular structure of the CDs/EP coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion monitoring behaviors of composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Given the sophistication and harshness of marine environment, steel structure/coating systems suffer from the coupling damage of external impact and corrosive erosion. To ascertain the corrosion monitoring efficacy of the prepared CDs/EP coating, cracks were initially created on the surface of the CDs/EP coating samples using the self-designed deflection device. In order to simulate the marine environment, the aforementioned coating samples were immersed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. As displayed in Fig. 9a, the fluorescence observed at the cracks of the coatings was markedly stronger than intact regions at the initial stage. With the passage of time, the fluorescence of the coatings gradually diminished until it was completely extinguished after 12 h of immersion, indicating that the synthesized composite coatings have the capability of corrosion warning. The specific mechanism is shown in Fig. 9b, the corrosive medium enters into the coating interior through cracks, and electrochemical corrosion occurs at the metal/coating interface in direct contact with the metal to generate Fe3+. Since the CDs/EP coating contains N-CDs, the functional groups on the surface of N-CDs that can generate fluorescence, such as -NH2, -COOH, and -OH will recognize Fe3+ and react with it in a complex reaction, which ultimately leads to fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the synthesized N-CDs can effectively recognize and warn of interfacial corrosion, and the prepared composite coatings exhibit corrosion warning capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of corrosion products
ce:section.ce:para: To gain further insight into the corrosion warning process and to remove the cracked coating, the morphology and elemental composition were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to assess the corrosion condition of the steel surface. As illustrated in Fig. 10a, the surface of the initial sample exhibited a relatively smooth texture, with the presence of sanding scratches. The corresponding elemental distribution and EDS maps demonstrate the presence of substantial quantities of Fe and C elements and a paucity of other heteroatoms, indicating that no corrosion has occurred in the steel at this time. [55] The presence of corrosion products in the 5 h immersion sample (Fig. 10b) indicates that the corrosive medium gains access to the interior of the coating through the cracks and comes into direct contact with the steel, thereby initiating corrosion at the interface. The elemental distribution and EDS maps demonstrate the presence of elevated concentrations of oxygen, sodium, and chloride elements, which originate from the corrosion reaction of the steel matrix and the influx of corrosive media. As illustrated in Fig. 10c, the samples exhibited a considerable quantity of corrosion products accompanied by corrosion pits after 12 h of immersion, indicating severe interfacial corrosion. The corresponding elemental distribution and EDS plots demonstrate that the concentration of oxygen is markedly higher in the samples that have been immersed for 12 h compared to those that have been immersed for 5 h. This indicates that the extent of interfacial corrosion of the steel increases with the prolongation of the immersion time. As the degree of steel corrosion increases, the concentration of Fe3+ iron ions rises, leading to enhanced complexation with N-CDs. These results in a notable fluorescence quenching phenomenon, manifested as a reduction in fluorescence intensity. In conclusion, the developed composite coating exhibits exceptional corrosion warning capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, the fluorescent properties of carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been employed to construct a smart coating that provides a graded damage warning for coating/steel systems. The smart coating was prepared by embedding carbon quantum dots (CDs) in an epoxy resin matrix. The occurrence of stress-induced cracking and subsequent extension of the coating will be indicated by an observable enhancement in fluorescence. The enhanced behavior of interfacial fluorescence indicates that the coating can be subjected to both transient and continuous stress, thereby providing an early warning of any damage to the coating. Concurrently, the occurrence of metal interfacial corrosion in the damaged region can be identified through the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Furthermore, the toughness and corrosion resistance of the coatings were enhanced by modifying the structure of the polymer molecular chains. Additionally, the failure of the metal/coating system under stress was evaluated in situ using an in situ mechano-electrochemical testing device. This smart coating, which possesses a delamination damage warning function and excellent mechanical properties, providing protection for metal parts in harsh environments and under stress. Consequently, it is anticipated that this innovative coating will prove an efficacious solution in the domain of corrosion protection.
